perturbation.original_text: For purposes of this Agreement, the following terms have the following meanings: (1) "Configuration" means the preparation of a computer and related ------------- hardware and integration of components into a computer system; provided -------- that the term "Configuration" shall not include installation of a computer or related hardware at a customer site.
perturbation.changed_text: For purposes of this Agreement, the following terms have the following meanings: (1) "Configuration" means the preparation of a computer and related ------------- hardware and integration of components into a computer system; provided -------- that the term "Configuration" shall include installation of a computer or related hardware at a customer site.
perturbation.explanation: By changing "shall not include" to "shall include", the definition of 'Configuration' now explicitly includes installation at the customer site, creating a conflict with later clauses that exclude on-site configuration from certain services.
perturbation.contradicted_law: The definition of 'computer installation' can vary across different states, impacting tax and regulatory compliance. Misclassifying it may lead to issues with state sales tax laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: This code defines property for depreciation purposes and relies on the classification of computer installations. The modified definition in the contract could conflict with how these installations are categorized for tax purposes, potentially affecting depreciation schedules and tax liabilities.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 26 U.S. Code § 168 - Accelerated cost recovery system | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 26 U.S. Code § 168 - Accelerated cost recovery system U.S. Code Notes Authorities (CFR) prev | next (a) General rule Except as otherwise provided in this section, the depreciation deduction provided by section 167(a) for any tangible property shall be determined by using— (1) the applicable depreciation method, (2) the applicable recovery period, and (3) the applicable convention. (b) Applicable depreciation method For purposes of this section— (1) In general Except as provided in paragraphs (2) and (3), the applicable depreciation method is— (A) the 200 percent declining balance method, (B) switching to the straight line method for the 1st taxable year for which using the straight line method with respect to the adjusted basis as of the beginning of such year will yield a larger allowance. (2) 150 percent declining balance method in certain cases Paragraph (1) shall be applied by substituting “150 percent” for “200 percent” in the case of— (A) any 15-year or 20-year property not referred to in paragraph (3), (B) any property (other than property described in paragraph (3)) which is a qualified smart electric meter or qualified smart electric grid system , or (C) any property (other than property described in paragraph (3)) with respect to which the taxpayer elects under paragraph (5) to have the provisions of this paragraph apply. (3) Property to which straight line method applies The applicable depreciation method shall be the straight line method in the case of the following property: (A) Nonresidential real property . (B) Residential rental property . (C) Any railroad grading or tunnel bore . (D) Property with respect to which the taxpayer elects under paragraph (5) to have the provisions of this paragraph apply. (E) Property described in subsection (e)(3)(D)(ii). (F) Water utilit
perturbation.justification: The change from "shall not include" to "shall include" directly alters the definition of 'Configuration.' According to the Perturbation Explanation, this creates a conflict because it contradicts later clauses that exclude on-site configuration from certain services. The Law Explanation suggests this reclassification could impact depreciation schedules and tax liabilities, relevant to 26 U.S.C. § 168, which addresses the accelerated cost recovery system. While the snippets themselves don't explicitly define 'Configuration' or its tax implications, they establish the legal framework for property depreciation, and the altered definition has the potential to misalign with established tax classifications, leading to non-compliance.

perturbation.original_text: (5) "Outsourcing and Professional Services" means (a) consulting, ------------------------------------- system migrations, project management, other services typically referred to as "high-end" services, and (b) outsourcing contracts having a term of more than one year which require pricing be done on a per seat basis.
perturbation.changed_text: (5) "Outsourcing and Professional Services" means services and contracts as defined by the service provider, and includes but is not limited to consulting, system migrations, project management, other services typically referred to as "high-end" services, and outsourcing contracts without regard to term length or pricing structure.
perturbation.explanation: By altering the definition of 'Outsourcing and Professional Services' to be at the discretion of the service provider, the contract introduces ambiguity. The law will often have specific standards for these services.
perturbation.contradicted_law: This contradicts standard definitions of professional services used by state licensing boards and departments of labor.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code generally regulates who can provide professional services and how they should be regulated. By changing the definitions of 'professional services' to be in the control of the service provider and disregarding the contract terms and pricing structure, this directly contradicts the intent of the Business and Professions Code.

HEALING ARTS [500 - 4999.129] ( Division 2 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 399. ) CHAPTER 1. General Provisions [500 - 865.2] ( Chapter 1 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 399. ) ARTICLE 1. Records [500 - 502] ( Article 1 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 399. ) 500. If the register or book of registration of the Medical Board of California, the Dental Board of California, or the California State Board of Pharmacy is destroyed by fire or other public calamity, the board, whose duty it is to keep the register or book, may reproduce it so that there may be shown as nearly as possible the record existing in the original at the time of destruction. (Amended by Stats. 2015, Ch. 426, Sec. 3. (SB 800) Effective January 1, 2016.)
perturbation.justification: The original contract defined 'Outsourcing and Professional Services' with specific criteria, including term length and pricing structure. The changed text allows the service provider to define these services without regard to these criteria. The Law Explanation states that this change contradicts the intent of the Business and Professions Code, because the Code regulates who can provide professional services. The changed text gives control of the definition of professional services to the service provider. Scraped Law Snippet 1, BPC 500, refers to the regulation of healing arts, and other professional services, implying that these services are subject to legal oversight, which the changed text undermines by granting definitional authority to the service provider. Therefore, the change creates a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: (7) "Services" means all IT services offered by Seller, including, -------- but not limited to, all outsourcing, professional services, break/fix, staff augmentation and consulting services; provided that the term -------- "Services" shall not include (i) on-site Configuration of Products by CompuCom or (ii) the sale (but not the performance) by CompuCom of extended warranty contracts at time of initial sale of Products to customers.
perturbation.changed_text: (7) "Services" means all IT services offered by Seller, including, -------- but not limited to, all outsourcing, professional services, break/fix, staff augmentation and consulting services; and the term -------- "Services" shall include (i) on-site Configuration of Products by CompuCom and (ii) the sale and performance by CompuCom of extended warranty contracts at time of initial sale of Products to customers.
perturbation.explanation: The modified definition of 'Services' now includes on-site configuration of products and the performance of extended warranty contracts, directly conflicting with the original exclusions.
perturbation.contradicted_law: This change can impact how service contracts are taxed and regulated under state laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: Texas Tax Code Section 151.0031 addresses taxable services. By including on-site configuration and extended warranty performance, the contract potentially reclassifies these activities as taxable services, which would have implications for sales tax collection and reporting.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: ODE TITLE 2. STATE TAXATION SUBTITLE E. SALES, EXCISE, AND USE TAXES CHAPTER 151. LIMITED SALES, EXCISE, AND USE TAX SUBCHAPTER A. GENERAL PROVISIONS Sec. 151.001. SHORT TITLE. This chapter may be cited as the Limited Sales, Excise, and Use Tax Act. Acts 1981, 67th Leg., p. 1545, ch. 389, Sec. 1, eff. Jan. 1, 1982. Sec. 151.002. APPLICABILITY OF DEFINITIONS, ETC. The definitions and other provisions of this chapter relating to the collection, administration, and enforcement of the taxes imposed by this chapter, including the requirements for sales tax permits, apply to the parties to a sale of a taxable item that is exempted from the taxes imposed by this chapter but that is subject to the taxes imposed by a city under Chapter 321 of this code. Acts 1981, 67th Leg., p. 1545, ch. 389, Sec. 1, eff. Jan. 1, 1982. Amended by Acts 1989, 71st Leg., ch. 2, Sec. 14.27(b)(1), eff. Aug. 28, 1989. Sec. 151.0028. "AMUSEMENT SERVICES". (a) "Amusement services" means the provision of amusement, entertainment, or recreation, but does not include the provision of educational or health services if prescribed by a licensed practitioner of the healing arts for the primary purpose of education or health maintenance or improvement. (b) "Amusement services" includes membership in a private club or organization that provides entertainment, recreational, sports, dining, or social facilities to its members. (c) "Amusement services" does not include services provided through coin-operated machines that are operated by the consumer. Added by Acts 1984, 68th Leg., 2nd C.S., ch. 31, art. 7, Sec. 3, eff. Oct. 2, 1984. Amended by Acts 1987, 70th Leg., 2nd C.S., ch. 5, art. 1, pt. 4, Sec. 1. Amended by: Acts 2019, 86th Leg., R.S., Ch. 638 (S.B. 1525 ), Sec. 1, eff. June 10, 2019. Sec. 151.003. "BUSINESS". "Business" means an activity of or caused by a person for the purpose of a direct or indirect gain, benefit, or advantage. Acts 1981, 67th Leg., p. 1545, ch. 389, Sec. 1, eff. Jan. 1, 1982. Sec. 151.0031. "COMPUTER PROGRAM". "Computer program" means a series of instructions that are coded for acceptance or use by a computer system and that are designed to permit the computer system to process data and provide results and information. The series of instructions may be contained in or on magnetic tapes, punched cards, printed instructions, or other tangible or electronic media. Added by Acts 1984, 68th Leg., 2nd C.S., ch. 31, art. 6, Sec. 1, eff. Oct. 2, 1984. Sec. 151.0033. "CABLE TELEVISION SERVICE". "Cable television service" means the distribution of video programming with or without use of wires to subscribing or paying customers. Added by Acts 1984, 68th Leg., 2nd C.S., ch. 31, art. 7, Sec. 4, eff. Oct. 2, 1984. Sec. 151.0034. "CREDIT REPORTING SERVICE". "Credit reporting service" means assembling or furnishing credit history or credit information relating to any person. Added by Acts 1987, 70th Leg., 2nd C.S., ch. 5, art. 1, pt. 4, Sec. 2. Sec. 151.0035. "DATA PROCESSING SERVICE". (a) "Data processing service" includes: (1) word processing, data entry, data retrieval, data search, information compilation, payroll and business accounting data production, and other computerized data and information storage or manipulation; (2) the performance of a totalisator service with the use of computational equipment required by Subtitle A-1, Title 13, Occupations Code (Texas Racing Act); and (3) the use of a computer or computer time for data processing whether the processing is performed by the provider of the computer or computer time or by the purchaser or other beneficiary of the service. (b) "Data processing service" does not include: (1) the transcription of medical dictation by a medical transcriptionist; (2) services exclusively to encrypt electronic payment information for acceptance onto a payment card network described by Subdivision (3)(E) to comply with standards set by the Payment Card Industry Security Standards Council; or (3) settling of an electronic pay
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts the original contract by including on-site configuration of products and the performance of extended warranty contracts in the definition of 'Services,' where previously these were explicitly excluded. The Law Explanation suggests this reclassification could have tax implications under Texas Tax Code Section 151.0031 regarding taxable services, although the provided snippet doesn't directly confirm that these services are taxable. However, the change directly reverses the original intent, creating a legally significant contradiction, as it alters the scope of 'Services' which likely impacts obligations related to that definition.

perturbation.original_text: 3.1 Seller's Agreement. (a) Seller agrees that during the Non- ------------------ Competition Period Seller will not, and will not permit any of its Subsidiaries to: (i) engage in the Product Business; (ii) encourage any Service Account to specify a party other than CompuCom to provide Products and image load/configurations to such Service Account; or (iii) solicit, entice or induce any employee of CompuCom or any Subsidiary of CompuCom to terminate his or her employment with CompuCom or any Subsidiary of CompuCom or hire any person who was or is at any time from the date of execution of the Asset Purchase Agreement to the end of the Non-Competition Period an employee of CompuCom or any Subsidiary of CompuCom.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.1 Seller's Agreement. (a) Seller agrees that during the Non- ------------------ Competition Period Seller will, and will permit any of its Subsidiaries to: (i) engage in the Product Business; (ii) encourage any Service Account to specify a party other than CompuCom to provide Products and image load/configurations to such Service Account; or (iii) solicit, entice or induce any employee of CompuCom or any Subsidiary of CompuCom to terminate his or her employment with CompuCom or any Subsidiary of CompuCom or hire any person who was or is at any time from the date of execution of the Asset Purchase Agreement to the end of the Non-Competition Period an employee of CompuCom or any Subsidiary of CompuCom.
perturbation.explanation: By changing "will not" to "will", the Seller's Agreement to not compete is completely reversed, fundamentally contradicting the non-compete clause.
perturbation.contradicted_law: This undermines the enforceability of the non-compete agreement under Delaware law.
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware Code Title 6, Section 2707 outlines acceptable restraints of trade and commerce. By effectively nullifying the non-compete agreement, the modified text permits actions that could be viewed as unreasonable restraints of trade, thus conflicting with the statute's intent to promote fair competition.

(a) All bonds, specialties, and notes in writing, payable to any person, or order, or assigns, may be assigned, or indorsed, and the assignee, or indorsee, his or her executors, administrators, or assigns, may again assign, or indorse the same, as often as desired. perturbation.justification: The change from "will not" to "will" directly reverses the seller's agreement to a non-compete clause. The Law Explanation suggests this could be viewed as an unreasonable restraint of trade, thus conflicting with fair competition. Scraped Law Snippet 1 from Delaware Code Title 6, Chapter 27, while not directly addressing non-compete clauses, generally covers contracts and trade within Delaware, which this contract modification could potentially violate if interpreted as an unreasonable restraint.

perturbation.original_text: 6.9 GOVERNING LAW. THE VALIDITY AND INTERPRETATION OF THIS AGREEMENT ------------- AND THE TERMS AND CONDITIONS SET FORTH HEREIN SHALL BE GOVERNED BY AND CONSTRUED IN ACCORDANCE WITH THE LAWS OF THE STATE OF DELAWARE APPLICABLE TO CONTRACTS MADE AND TO BE PERFORMED WHOLLY THEREIN, WITHOUT GIVING EFFECT TO ANY PROVISIONS THEREOF RELATING TO CONFLICTS OF LAW.
perturbation.changed_text: 6.9 GOVERNING LAW. THE VALIDITY AND INTERPRETATION OF THIS AGREEMENT ------------- AND THE TERMS AND CONDITIONS SET FORTH HEREIN SHALL BE GOVERNED BY AND CONSTRUED IN ACCORDANCE WITH THE LAWS OF THE STATE OF CALIFORNIA, WITHOUT GIVING EFFECT TO ANY PROVISIONS THEREOF RELATING TO CONFLICTS OF LAW.
perturbation.explanation: By switching the governing law from Delaware to California, there can be conflict with the entire contract's validity. Delaware and California have very different approaches to the enforceability of non-compete agreements, and the application of California law in this instance could render the contract unenforceable.
perturbation.contradicted_law: This change potentially violates the Delaware choice-of-law rules if the original agreement was valid under Delaware law but is not under California law.
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware Code Title 6, Section 2708 pertains to choice of law. If the parties specifically agreed to Delaware law and the contract was executed in a manner consistent with Delaware law, unilaterally changing the governing law to California introduces uncertainty and could be challenged as a violation of the original contractual intent and Delaware public policy.

perturbation.justification: The change in governing law from Delaware to California directly contradicts the original intent of the contract. The Law Explanation states that changing the governing law unilaterally "introduces uncertainty and could be challenged as a violation of the original contractual intent and Delaware public policy." While Scraped Law Snippet 1 doesn't explicitly forbid changing the governing law, it outlines general contract principles in Delaware, implying stability and adherence to the agreed-upon jurisdiction. Switching to California law, as highlighted in the Perturbation Explanation, creates a conflict, particularly regarding enforceability, and undermines the initial legal framework chosen by both parties. This falls under the Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction definition, where contractual language (governing law) deviates from legally recognized intent, making the contract potentially unenforceable in its modified form.
